mechanism_description: Retiring fossil-fueled power plants reduces fossil electricity generation, thereby cutting power-sector greenhouse gas emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Fossil power retirement → ↓ fossil electricity generation → ↓ CO₂e emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: The city/utility retires, shuts down, or prevents continued operation of coal/oil/gas power plants (often paired with commitments not to repower or extend plant life).  
(2) Mediating physical process: Less combustion of fossil fuels for electricity reduces stack emissions (primarily CO₂, plus smaller amounts of CH₄/N₂O).  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower grid-supplied electricity emissions intensity and absolute power-sector GHG emissions, contributing to reduced cumulative emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced greenhouse gas emissions from the electricity sector by phasing out fossil-fired generation.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: retiring fossil plants reduces fossil generation and directly cuts GHG emissions from electricity production.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while retirements can also improve air quality, health, and sometimes resilience, those are co-benefits; the mechanism’s defining and largest climate effect is emissions reduction.  
3) Inherent causal chain for each secondary: none—resource efficiency, adaptation, or nature-based outcomes are not physically guaranteed by plant retirement alone.  
4) Edge cases applied: if fossil retirement is replaced by imported fossil electricity, diesel peakers, or increased gas generation elsewhere, net mitigation could be reduced, but the mechanism as stated targets phaseout and emissions reduction in the power mix.  
5) Confirm labels apply to ALL instances: any policy whose mechanism is decommissioning fossil power to phase out fossil generation has mitigation as its primary, mechanism-intrinsic climate objective and effect.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Net emissions benefits depend on what replaces retired generation (clean replacement vs. shifting fossil generation to other units/regions) and on system reliability measures (e.g., temporary peakers); those contingencies affect magnitude, not the mechanism’s primary classification.
co_benefits: Improved local air quality (NOx/SO₂/PM reductions) and associated health benefits; potential reduction in local thermal pollution if relevant to plant cooling discharges.
instance_edge_case_notes: Instrument type is ambiguous from the minimal text (could be a regulatory mandate/phaseout schedule, procurement requirement, or integrated resource planning directive). Coded as “planning-strategy” because it is framed as a strategy statement rather than a specified enforceable instrument.
